Arithmetic.title: Arithmetic Aptitude
Arithmetic.overview: Arithmetic aptitude tests your ability to solve basic mathematical problems. Select a sub-topic to see detailed formulas.

Percentage.title: 1. What is a Percentage?
Percentage.content:
The 'Out of 100' Concept

Percentage is simply a fraction where the denominator is always 100. It helps us compare things of different sizes easily.

Basic Formula: (Value / Total) × 100

Example: If you get 40 marks out of 50, your percentage is (40/50) × 100 = 80%.

Percentage.title: 2. Fraction-Percentage Table
Percentage.content:
The Beginner's Shortcut

Memorizing these will help you solve problems in seconds without a calculator:

  • 1/2 = 50%
  • 1/4 = 25%
  • 1/5 = 20%
  • 1/8 = 12.5%
  • 1/10 = 10%

Percentage.title: 3. Price vs Consumption
Percentage.content:
Keeping the Budget Balanced

If the price of an item increases by R%, you must reduce how much you buy to keep your spending the same.

Reduction Formula: [R / (100 + R)] × 100%

Example: If price rises by 25%, you must reduce consumption by [25/125] × 100 = 20%.

Percentage.title: 4. The Net Change Trick
Percentage.content:
Successive Changes

When a value changes twice in a row (like two discounts or two price hikes), use this formula:

Net Change = [x + y + (xy/100)]%

Note: Use negative numbers for discounts/decreases and positive for increases.

Profit & Loss.title: The Fundamentals
Profit & Loss.content:
Profit and Loss deal with the buying and selling prices of commodities. Understanding these terms is the first step:

  • Cost Price (CP): The price at which an item is purchased.
  • Selling Price (SP): The price at which an item is sold.
  • Marked Price (MP): The price listed on the tag by the shopkeeper.

Profit occurs when SP > CP, and Loss occurs when CP > SP.

Profit & Loss.title: Important Profit & Loss Formulas
Profit & Loss.content:
1. Profit = SP - CP
2. Loss = CP - SP
3. Profit% = (Profit / CP) × 100
4. Loss% = (Loss / CP) × 100

Warning: Profit and Loss percentages are always calculated on the Cost Price (CP) unless stated otherwise in the question.

Profit & Loss.title: Discounts and Successive Discounts
Profit & Loss.content:
Discounts are always calculated on the Marked Price (MP).

  • Selling Price (SP) = MP - Discount
  • Discount % = (Discount / MP) × 100

Shortcut for Successive Discounts:
If a shopkeeper offers two successive discounts of x% and y%, the equivalent single discount is:
(x + y - xy/100)%

Simple Interest.title: Introduction to Simple Interest
Simple Interest.content:
Simple Interest (SI) is a quick method of calculating the interest charge on a loan. It is determined by multiplying the daily interest rate by the principal by the number of days that elapse between payments.


The Master Formula:
SI = (P × R × T) / 100

Where:
P = Principal Amount
R = Annual Interest Rate (%)
T = Time Period (Years)

Simple Interest.title: Total Amount and Variations
Simple Interest.content:
The Total Amount (A) returned to the lender includes both the principal and the interest earned.

Amount (A) = P + SI
or
A = P [1 + (RT/100)]

Time Factor Tip: If the time is given in months, divide by 12 to convert to years. If given in days, divide by 365.

Time & Distance.title: The Speed-Time-Distance Relationship
Time & Distance.content:
This is the foundation of all problems involving motion. The three variables are linked by one simple equation:

Distance = Speed × Time

Unit Conversion (The 5/18 Rule):
  • To convert km/hr to m/s: Multiply by 5/18.
  • To convert m/s to km/hr: Multiply by 18/5.

Time & Distance.title: Relative Speed and Trains
Time & Distance.content:
Relative speed is the speed of one object with respect to another.

  • Same Direction: Relative Speed = |S1 - S2|
  • Opposite Direction: Relative Speed = S1 + S2

Train Crossing Logic:
1. When a train crosses a stationary pole/man, the distance covered equals the Length of the Train.
2. When a train crosses a bridge/platform, the distance covered equals (Length of Train + Length of Platform).

Time & Work.title: Concepts of Work and Efficiency
Time & Work.content:
Time and work problems depend on the relationship between the time taken to complete a job and the rate of work (efficiency).

  • If A completes a task in n days, his 1-day work is 1/n.
  • Efficiency is inversely proportional to time. If A is twice as efficient as B, A will take half the time B takes.

Time & Work.title: The Group Work Formula (Chain Rule)
Time & Work.content:
When multiple people work together for different hours or days, use the Man-Days Formula:

(M1 × D1 × H1) / W1 = (M2 × D2 × H2) / W2

Where:
M = No. of Workers
D = Days
H = Hours per day
W = Total Work or Wages earned

Average.title: Understanding Averages
Average.content:
An average (or mean) is the central value of a set of data. It is the ratio of the sum of all observations to the total number of observations.


Average = Sum of Observations / Number of Observations

The Replacement Rule:
If a new person joins a group and the average increases by 'x', the weight/age of the new person = (Old Average) + (New Total Strength × x).

HCF & LCM.title: HCF and LCM Concepts
HCF & LCM.content:
• HCF (Highest Common Factor): The greatest number that exactly divides two or more numbers. Also known as GCD.
  • LCM (Least Common Multiple): The smallest number which is exactly divisible by two or more numbers.

Crucial Rule for Two Numbers:
HCF(a,b) × LCM(a,b) = a × b

Number System.title: Classification of Numbers
Number System.content:
A number system is a way of representing numbers. Standard types include:

  • Natural Numbers: 1, 2, 3...
  • Whole Numbers: 0, 1, 2, 3...
  • Integers: ...-2, -1, 0, 1, 2...
  • Prime Numbers: Numbers with exactly two factors (e.g., 2, 3, 5, 7, 11). 2 is the only even prime.

Ages.title: Solving Age Problems
Ages.content:
Problems on ages are essentially linear equations. The key is to correctly set up the variables based on the timeframe.

  • If current age is X, then age n years ago was X - n.
  • If current age is X, then age n years from now will be X + n.

Important Note: The difference between the ages of two people remains constant throughout their lives.

Probability.title: Fundamentals of Probability
Probability.content:
Probability is the branch of mathematics concerning numerical descriptions of how likely an event is to occur.


The Probability Formula:
P(E) = n(E) / n(S)

Where:
n(E) = Number of favorable outcomes
n(S) = Total number of possible outcomes in the Sample Space.

Reasoning.title: Logical Reasoning
Reasoning.overview: Logical reasoning helps in developing analytical skills through patterns and puzzles.

Number Series.title: Patterns
Number Series.content: Series can follow Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division, Squares, or Cubes pattern.

Coding-Decoding.title: Letter Shifting
Coding-Decoding.content: Letters are shifted forward/backward. Tip: Write A-Z with positions 1-26.

Coding-Decoding.title: Opposite Letters
Coding-Decoding.content: Pairs that sum to 27: A-Z, B-Y, C-X, D-W (EV, FU, GT, HS, IR, JQ, KP, LO, MN).

Blood Relation.title: Family Tree Symbols
Blood Relation.content: Male: (+), Female: (-), Couple: (=), Siblings: (—), Generations: vertical line (|).

Blood Relation.title: Relations
Blood Relation.content: Maternal: From Mother's side. Paternal: From Father's side. In-laws: By marriage.

Calendar.title: Leap Years
Calendar.content: Divisible by 4. Century years must be divisible by 400 (e.g., 2000 is leap, 1900 is not).

Calendar.title: Odd Days
Calendar.content: Ordinary year (365 days) = 1 odd day. Leap year (366 days) = 2 odd days. 100 years = 5 odd days, 400 years = 0 odd days.

Direction.title: Cardinal Points
Direction.content: Main: North, South, East, West. Sub-directions: NE, NW, SE, SW.

Direction.title: Pythagoras Rule
Direction.content: Shortest distance c = √(a² + b²), where a and b are perpendicular paths.

Clock.title: Mirror Image
Clock.content: Mirror Time = 11:60 - Actual Time (if time is between 1 and 11). Otherwise use 23:60.

Syllogism.title: Venn Diagrams
Syllogism.content: All A are B: A is inside B. Some A are B: A and B intersect. No A is B: A and B are separate.

Syllogism.title: Conclusions
Syllogism.content: A conclusion is valid only if it holds true in all possible Venn diagram cases.

Seating Arrangement.title: Circular
Seating Arrangement.content: Facing center: Right is anticlockwise, Left is clockwise. Facing out: Opposite.

Seating Arrangement.title: Linear
Seating Arrangement.content: Facing North: Your left is their left. Facing South: Your left is their right.

Analogy.title: Types
Analogy.content: Word Analogy (Country:Capital), Number Analogy (4:16 :: 5:25), Letter Analogy.

Odd One Out.title: Classification
Odd One Out.content: Grouping by shared properties. Pick the one that doesn't share the property (e.g., Prime vs Composite).

Statement & Conclusion.title: Rules
Statement & Conclusion.content: Conclusion must follow directly from statement. Avoid assumptions outside the statement.

Verbal.title: Verbal Ability
Verbal.overview: Verbal ability tests language proficiency and comprehension.

Synonyms.title: Basics
Synonyms.content: Words with similar meanings. Example: Abandon - Forsake, Abundant - Plentiful.

Antonyms.title: Basics
Antonyms.content: Words with opposite meanings. Example: Ancient - Modern, Arrogant - Humble.

Fill in the Blanks.title: Strategies
Fill in the Blanks.content: 1. Check grammar (Tense, Article). 2. Identify the tone (Positive/Negative). 3. Look for context clues.

Idioms & Phrases.title: Examples
Idioms & Phrases.content:
A piece of cake: Very easy.
Spill the beans: Reveal a secret.
Under the weather: Feeling sick.

One Word Substitution.title: People/Groups
One Word Substitution.content:
Atheist: One who doesn't believe in God.
Philanthropist: Lover of mankind.
Altruist: One who works for others.

Reading Comprehension.title: Methods
Reading Comprehension.content: Skimming: Quick reading for gist. Scanning: Looking for specific info. SQ3R: Survey, Question, Read, Recite, Review.

Sentence Correction.title: S-V Agreement
Sentence Correction.content: Singular subject takes singular verb. 'Each', 'Every', 'Either' are singular.

Sentence Correction.title: Tenses
Sentence Correction.content: Ensure consistency in tense throughout the sentence.

Spelling Test.title: Common Errors
Spelling Test.content: Words with double consonants (Occurred, Committee) or tricky vowels (Receive, Believe).
